content.problemStatement: You are given two integers NNN and KKK. You may perform the following operation any number of times (including zero): change NNN to N−KN-KN−K, i.e. subtract KKK from NNN. Find the smallest non-negative integer value of NNN you can obtain this way.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated integers N and K.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the smallest non‑negative integer that can be obtained by repeatedly subtracting K from N.

solution.approach: If K is zero, subtracting does nothing, so the value stays N. Otherwise we can subtract K as many times as possible while keeping the result non‑negative, which leaves the remainder of N divided by K. Hence the answer is N % K when K > 0, else N.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few variables are used.

code.Python3:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        K = int(next(it))
        ans = N if K == 0 else N % K
        out.append(str(ans))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
